speaker: interviewer
text: And this is tape five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell me about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My sister did take care of the Swiss for the offices. And she was not allowed, really. She was helping in the  kitchen-- you know, like wash the dishes. Help a little, but was not allowed to go  herself. That was my-- I told Schultz, I don't want her to go into.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, we did have a Jewish man and woman that were assigned to the kitchen-- young couple. They were just married. In a-- in the-- in the war barracks, you know. And they were helping. And one day, we prepared big parties. And who is walking in? The Sturmbannfuhrer Keitel with a Ukrainian girl. And the major told that there is a Polish girl that is working there. So she came flying with, oh, oh, so nice to meet you, and this, in Polish. Oh, I am here. I wanted to be friends with you, and this. And then she looks at the Jewish man. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was trying to know, in a book I have, his name. He's looking at him, and he said, what are you doing here? And he said, he said, you can poison. You work in kitchen? You can poison all the officers here. And the, and the Sturmbannfuhrer Keitel. He did have big, big eyes. He said-- she started the scream. Schultz walked in, and he said fraulein, we set the dinner. Would you please go out? I felt like to take her by, by the head and really punch in the nose. Where would we find out? He was in the-- starting in school with that girl. And she wanted him to marry her. You know, she was a pretty woman, and he-- and he instead-- you know, that was before the war. And he refused. And so she recognized him. So now she did have a-- Schultz could not keep them, send them back to the-- to the camp. And that's the last time I did see that young couple.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was a party. And they needed help. Now, Janina, Schultz asked her to help to serve. She wear a just a plain black dress, but she had the most beautiful gold hair. And she looked so tall and beautiful. As soon as she walked in, he's noticed her, right away, Sturmbannfuhrer Keitel. I thought that he  work his hand. He stood up. He-- he-- this-- with his legs, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kicked his heels.

speaker: interviewee
text: Heels. And I mean-- I mean, he just spotted-- he said, who is that beauty? Major said, well, she's-- this is the sister of Irene. So I excused, I said, Herr Major, we have so much work to do. You know. Then he start to come the next day and next day. And he insisted that she's serving him. Now, Janina knew what we were doing, that I was helping in the laundry, she was helping there. And he tried to invite her to go out, tried to take her out. Well, I knew that she is so young. She's younger than myself. He gave her to drink. He-- you know. I did not want her to be with a Gestapo man. So I now went to Major. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the major's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edward Rugamer. And I went to him, and I said, I am oldest. I am responsible for my sisters. Sturmbannfuhrer Keitel throw himself. I don't want it. She's young. I don't want her to-- he has  many women. He-- I don't want it to spoil her life. Please, help me. Get me permit that she can go back to my aunt to Radom. He did that for me. But he asked, but you will remain. Say yes, I will stay with you to the end. So with crying and this, my sister left. I sent her off. Now I have only Helen. I didn't have-- you know, she was not with me, but at least I have somebody to talk. Helen was talking to my sister, saying, you must leave. You will endanger Helen, endanger everybody. You know, you don't want it. So she left. And I will remain alone. Now I start hearing in the conversation the German when I was serving that the Russians were really, really giving them a hard time. And the major said, well, if they will come closer, the more soldiers will come, more officers. So he would look for place. That, from the conversation. Now I overheard that the end is coming. A few weeks, or three or four weeks later, I heard Sturmbannfuhrer Keitel saying, I am your friend to the major. You better get some other workers-- Ukrainian and Polish. We will teach them him-- how to work because the Jews, soon they will not be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when I heard that and I told to my friends, they were asking me, Irene, help. What can I do? I did not have a home. I was not with my family. But I knew, if I don't help, they will be dead. There's nothing I could do. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a miracle happened for me. God put me in the right place at the right time, because I overheard the major saying that more soldiers are coming and that he's looking for home. Soon, he told me that he found a villa, a big home. And he wanted me to go to translate it, because they were Polish and Ukranian. There were-- on one floor, one family live, and on the other, there's-- so he was telling me to go translate it from Polish to, to, to German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he told them that they have to move out and-- because he wants to take it. If they don't, they will be thrown out. If they decide that they will move, he will even help them to move them. So I told that translator. When I came to the laundry room, I told him. I said, look, there is a place. There is a place. You know. Let's pray there will be a place. But the time took so long that six from the people that were in the laundry did not want to wait. I took them to the forest. Only six left now in the laundry room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the time was coming short. The Polish family moved out. The Ukrainians spoke to Major. And he gave them a grace for a couple of days yet to move. In the meantime, the date was set that this and this is the last day that the Jews will come to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you how you managed to take them to the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not easy, but Helen did have a sled and a horse, or the wagon and a horse. And Major sometimes left for two or three days on the different jobs. So I was taking a little ride and picking the people. They were hiding in, in the edge of the forest or in the park or in someplace, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they ran away individually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the laundry room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from the laundry room. From the laundry room, yes. They went to the barracks, the work barracks. Between the work barracks and the laundry room, they disappeared. You know, at that time, Rokita was very busy going to all different villages and doing all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many of them-- and some of them, Helen helped to, to take them. They were hidden in a-- in a wagon. There were hay and potatoes and the whole thing. It looked like a farmer that is going to town. And that's how we were working out. But now the time is coming that those 12 did have to-- I could not move them to the villa because the couple was there, and the time was coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I did, I hide them. It was six now only. I hide them behind the walls in the laundry room. I never forget the-- they hide. And the next day, they will not go out anymore, because that was supposed to be-- the-- and the morning come. And the Gestapo will come to pick up and come to Schultz and ask where the-- where the people are from the-- from the offices and from the laundry room. And I said, well-- them-- I don't know. I locked the laundry room. Nobody there. And I say, if you want to go see-- you know-- so Schultz with me walked in. I opened the door. But they were not really looking for-- they trusted, because Schultz said, they're my slaves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they opened with everything fine, and they left. And they were led-- but they were looking and finding people hiding in the factories here and there, taking them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they were going to death. I knew. There's nothing anybody could do now. The Gestapo was there and taking them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I did not-- I hide them, but I did not know that after they take all the Jews from places that they were working, then the Gestapo comes and searches really the German premises, because the Germans did have the factories. I was serving the dinner. I heard the young secretary's German. One said to the other, you better dress tomorrow nice, because there will be handsome SS men, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, well, what will they do? She said, well, they will search. You know, the Jews are hiding, you know. Like, this is a war, you know. When I heard that, I said, oh, my god. Now what will I do? You know, doesn't only looked in, but when they come search, they know how to search. So then the evening comes, and everybody was dressed, because a big company from Berlin comes to entertain the officers. So the secretary were all dressed, and Major is dressed. And Schultz is leaving too. You know. He said that they have a performance to look. And he said they will be back. So, so, so I was left in the little room by the diner by myself. When they left, two officers were sick, did not go. There were now-- major's apartment was on the third floor, and one was on the fourth floor. And one was on the second floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, finally when everything quiet down, I did go to the laundry room, did not make light, but told them, look, this and this situation is. I don't know because those, those people just-- the Ukrainian just moved today. Just were moving tonight. But they didn't. I went there to check. They did not. So-- and I could not leave them there. I brought them to the major's on the third floor, little by little. I didn't know where to put them. I could not in my little room. I cleaned Major's apartments always. Over the bathtub was a little opening, like maybe four by five, you know, the air vent. There was a very heavy walls, and they have an air vent that goes from one place over the kitchen floor all the way to the other side. I didn't know where to hide them. And for some reason, at that moment, I look at that. I took a chair, I open that, and I did see, there is this. So I brought the three men first, you know-- were sick, and-- to look at. It was wood. That was lucky, not metal, because it would make a noise. I put them there, six of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside a vent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the vent, all the way from bathtub all the way through the kitchen, over the kitchen to the other side. Well, there was by that time-- it was about 1 o'clock. And I said, now I don't know, maybe he's not coming sooner. I said, you have to be very quiet, and the major is an old man, because he was. He wear that-- he did not hear so good, and very heavy glasses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say, in the morning, I come. So I was coming down. And as soon as I walk in the house, in my room, I heard the Germans coming back from the party, drunken and singing and slurring and this, and the major too. OK? So the major did go up to sleep. In the morning-- I mean, I did not sleep that whole night. I, I-- during the night, yet, I went there, and I made it-- put the brooms and everything behind where was the smaller-- you know, made it like a storage place for-- so if the Gestapo would come, they'd say, that was a storage place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the morning, I was serving the breakfast. I heard like cockroaches, the Gestapo was every place, searching, looking here, there, you know. My feet were just like rubber. I could not wait enough for them to eat and go out. So as soon as the major left and everybody, I took a pail with, with-- put it-- something to drink, something to eat, and told Schultz that I go clean Major's apartment. I was walking in. The doorway opened. I said, Major usually closes the door, but the door opened. And as I was coming closer to the bathroom, young SS man coming out. You know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I don't look Jewish, you know. So at least-- he looked at me, and I was-- I said, well, I'm sorry, in German, I did not-- I was surprised in Major's apartment, you know. I am coming to clean his room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he say-- saluted and danke schoen, fraulein, and he go out. I opened the win-- the door. Ida was sitting like a Buddha, like this. You can see her through the-- because they were hearing, because they heard the whole night explosions, fires from the ghetto site, you know. And he just-- he was waiting for me to come. He went from his need, he needed pee-pee, I guess, you know. So he did his business and walked out. But for me, I locked the door and tell them that now that the Ukrainian moved, this night, I will take them out to the villa. OK? I did not think out how I will do it yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I-- you know-- I mean, I wanted help. But I was doing that, you don't have time to think. You do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, the night comes. You know, I locked the door. I was-- locked front and this, so they can come out for a second, go to the-- to the toilet. And I brought in some drink and this. Ida said, we will go out. We will give ourselves up, you know. You will lose your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, my life is not more worthy than you. We got so far. Go up there. Tonight you'll be, you know, in, in a villa. And so, everybody was tired because they went to bed late. Major was tired. When he went to-- was going to bed, he said that he has a big headache and he wanted to get something, some pills, I guess, was-- maybe for sleep, maybe for-- well, I brought him milk for drinking. I knew where he has a key. About an hour later when he was fast asleep, I took the key, I locked the door, his door. I locked him in. And I don't know why, but I remember I did that. And did have to let one by one down, and the three flights down, into the-- through the officers' entrance, because they could not go the other way. I let them into the night. And they knew where the villa is. And that's how they did go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was the villa from the old place?

speaker: interviewee
text: About, oh, not quite two blocks, you know. Not too far. It was a beautiful building, two story, with a third was the cellar, which was the servants' quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they escaped from the laundry room, or from the camp, were they searching for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so. But that was a Schultz business, you know, not mine. You know? I mean, the Schultz said, probably they left with the other transport because they were taking not everybody together. They come this and we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Schultz suspect that you have something to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess that Schultz knew something, that I am trying to help, that I am-- but like I say, he never-- he didn't  want to know. He never spoke about. But now is that villa. And the servant quarters, the first floor, there was a big entry hall and small entry hall by the door. And then a big entry hall that is to the cellar, to the servant quarters. And then there are stairs to go to the upper. From the big entry hall, there was a kitchen and another room with a kitchen. On that side was a dining-- formal dining room. And also like a living room or whatever, you know. And upstairs there were about four bedrooms. A big, big home. And so when I went-- and I did see that. So I said, well, God put me in the right place at the right time. That's the reason I open-- I went there, I opened the window to the German major villa to the coal chute. You know, they putting coal through the window. And I left that open. And they came through the coal chute down to the cellar.

speaker: interviewer
text: One by one?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the night. And they instead six, there were 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because some-- some people, you know, have a family, have a this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I expected there will be only six when I came to the villa. And how it happened, in the morning, the major said that he wanted me to go to the villa, that I will not serve breakfast or luncheon, only dinner. And he said, in the laundry room, you know, you have to-- the new crew will come. So you will teach them, tell them what is to be done. And I say, new? Why? What's happened? Did I do something wrong? You know. He said, oh no. That's what it is, you know. He did not even want to tell me what. So now, I am-- I went like a fly, so quick to the villa. And when I opened the-- and Major said, you have to go to the villa because I am sending a crew, soldiers, to paint the villa. I said, oh, my God. I did not know that he wants to do-- but the villa was dirty, because the people were leaving. And so, when I walked in, instead of six, 12 people look at me, you know, like children. And there is no time to get befriended or anything. And I was thinking, what I will do? They will-- So I lock them in the attic. I figure out that they will not paint the attic. They started with this. So I put it-- it was very hot. It was June. It was very, very hot. And there was only a little window by the ceiling. I felt like stepmother, like bad person to take the people and lock them there. I brought some water. There was a pail, and pail for waste, and some towel, one or two. And I say, I will come as soon as I can.

speaker: interviewee
text: And soon after the, like, four soldiers came, and they were-- selecting the color, and they were painting and this. And I was sitting like Mother Goose on the eggs. I was scared to-- to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister was gone by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister did go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, I living by the diner, in the big-- in, in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The old place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Till I moved to the villa. But there, I did have-- right by the diner was a little room for ceremonies, I guess, and I did have that room. Now, when you're with my sister, about a few days, maybe a week after my sister left, the, the-- Rokita, hey, where is your sister, they ask me? You know, he was mad. And just like from nowhere, I say, well, she did have tuberculosis and was sent-- and he turned white, you know. [LAUGHS] Just popped out. You see how God sometimes puts this-- I said, she did have a tuberculosis, and the major thought that it is-- she had to send her, because that is very infectious. And he did not bother me anymore. That was with him now. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually you moved back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am moving to the villas, right away, because the major said, look, you will serve only dinner. And I want you to overlook the painting and the cleaning and this. Schultz brought me a little cot for sleeping. He brought-- and he went back and forth with the car. He brought me-- so you don't need to run, he brought me towels, and he brought me to heat my water, and brought some cookies and bread and fruit. He say that you-- when you get hungry, you know. And so there was-- at night, Major did not move yet, because he wanted everything be cleaned and this. So at night, I-- the light-- we, we didn't make it light. The people could come down, stretch their legs, wash themselves, clean, and eat. And then-- now, finally downstairs was painted, down-- cellar and the kitchen and living room and this. Now, they did have only the bedrooms to do. So I took them down to the-- to the servant quarters. And I was thank-- I was so happy that I have a place for my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough food for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was never a problem, because not only that I was serving, you know, in the, the major-- in-- dinner yet. So Schultz was giving me lots of food. But the major, you know, stocked his house with food. The Schultz did go with me to the German warehouses and we bought whatever was needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop for a moment

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll talk--
